coredata.dc:title: Panax notoginseng powder -assisted preparation of carbon-quantum-dots/BiOCl with enriched oxygen vacancies and boosted photocatalytic performance
coredata.dc:creator: Liao, Hongru
Ran, Yu
Zhong, Junbo
Li, Jianzhang
Li, Minjiao
Yang, Hao
coredata.dc:description: Low activity of photocatalysts is a serious bottleneck to the practical application of photocatalytic technology. In this paper, a series of BiOCl composite photocatalysts containing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were successfully prepared by adding Panax notoginseng powder (PNP) to the solvothermal synthesis system of BiOCl as a template agent and a raw material for 0D CQDs. CQDs/BiOCl exhibit 2D flake structures and 3D flower-like microspheres self-assembled from thin flakes, holding rich oxygen vacancies (OVs). After detailed characterization, it was found that the amount of OVs on BiOCl could be regulated according to the amount of PNP added. The CQDs/OVs-BiOCl photocatalysts exhibit higher photogenerated charge separation efficiency and photocatalytic activity than the bare BiOCl. When the mass ratio of PNP/BiOCl is 1.0%, the photocatalyst demonstrates the maximum degradation activity for rhodamine B (RhB) and perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA). In view of the solid observations, a photocatalytic enhancement mechanism of CQDs/BiOCl was elucidated.

head.ce:title: Panax notoginseng powder -assisted preparation of carbon-quantum-dots/BiOCl with enriched oxygen vacancies and boosted photocatalytic performance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Low activity of photocatalysts is a serious bottleneck to the practical application of photocatalytic technology. In this paper, a series of BiOCl composite photocatalysts containing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were successfully prepared by adding Panax notoginseng powder (PNP) to the solvothermal synthesis system of BiOCl as a template agent and a raw material for 0D CQDs. CQDs/BiOCl exhibit 2D flake structures and 3D flower-like microspheres self-assembled from thin flakes, holding rich oxygen vacancies (OVs). After detailed characterization, it was found that the amount of OVs on BiOCl could be regulated according to the amount of PNP added. The CQDs/OVs-BiOCl photocatalysts exhibit higher photogenerated charge separation efficiency and photocatalytic activity than the bare BiOCl. When the mass ratio of PNP/BiOCl is 1.0%, the photocatalyst demonstrates the maximum degradation activity for rhodamine B (RhB) and perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA). In view of the solid observations, a photocatalytic enhancement mechanism of CQDs/BiOCl was elucidated.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Preparation of highly active photocatalysts is the core of advancing the practical application of photocatalytic technology (Hassan et al., 2022; Ge et al., 2022; Cui et al., 2022). bib_Liao_et_al_2021Liao et al. (2021a,b) and Xiong et al. (2022) proposed the same opinion that the intrinsic properties of photocatalysts essentially determine the photocatalytic efficiency. Among the massive photocatalysts, BiOCl has fascinated considerable attentions from the researchers in photocatalysis field. According to the reports of Li et al. (2018), Xiong et al. (2021), Long et al. (2022) and Hong et al. (2022), the reason is that the unique lamellar structure of BiOCl facilitates the separation of photoinduced carriers. However, Z. Xu et al. (2022), Wang et al., 2018 and Ma et al. (2022) found that the photocatalytic activity of BiOCl was still restricted by its limited utilization efficiency of visible light and rapid photoinduced charge recombination rate. Therefore, effectively solving these two issues is the important link to improve the activity of BiOCl. To resolve these two matters, researchers often adopt some various and practicable methods, such as morphology control (Cao et al., 2022; Matussin et al., 2022; Yadav et al., 2022), fabrication of composite materials (Y. Li et al., 2021; Huang et al., 2022; Bardos et al., 2021), introduction of OVs and so on (Dong et al., 2022; Zhong et al., 2022; Qin et al., 2022).

Morphology control of photocatalysts can affect the photocatalytic performance by altering the light utilization efficiency and photogenerated charge recombination efficiency. Considering the layered structure of BiOCl, converting the stacked form of BiOCl from 2D to 3D can induce multiple mirror-like reflections of light in the photocatalyst, thereby improving the light utilization efficiency. In addition, the 3D morphology can also reduce the recombination rate of photogenerated charges. Senasu et al. (2022) used polymeric surfactant PVP to assist the preparation of BiOBr with flower-like structure to achieve rapid decomposition of fluoroquinolone antibiotics under visible light. Y. Zhang et al. (2022a), 2022b, 2022c reported that the microstructure of BiOX (X = Cl, Br) can be tuned by dopamine for efficient photocatalysis under visible light. We used a reactable ionic liquid to fabricate flower-like BiOBr/BiOCl heterojunctions to achieve efficient degradation of RhB (Liao et al., 2021a,b). Yuan et al. (2022) reported that Bi2Sn2O7 can be formed into microspheres by doping with metal ions Tb3+ and Eu3+. Chen et al. (2022a), 2022b adopted spiral carbon fiber as a hard template agent to prepare 3D BiOCl for highly efficient photocatalytic detoxification of typical pollutants.

Coupling with functional materials is an important strategy to endow photocatalysts with the special functions. Kim et al. (2021) reported that they used zeolite to endow the zeolite/TiO2 with more active sites and adsorption capacity, which significantly improved the photocatalytic degradation and viral-inactivation properties of TiO2. To accelerate the separation of photoinduced carriers, materials with conductive properties, such as metals (Nie et al., 2021; Z. Liu et al., 2022b), MXene (S. Li et al., 2022) and CQDs (Zheng et al., 2022; Qi et al., 2022; Ming et al., 2022) are often selected. The article reported by T. Zhang et al. (2022c) claims that CQDs have up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL) property, which can be excited by low-energy light, emitting high -energy light. If coupling CQDs with photocatalyst, it is anticipated that the utilization of light will be significantly boosted.

Introduction of OVs in oxygen-containing photocatalysts is an important measure to achieve their visible-light driving and the improvement of the photocatalytic efficiency. At present, many strategies for fabrication of OVs have been reported. Chen et al. (2017) and Qin et al. (2021a), 2021b reported the successful introduction of OVs by thermal reduction of BiFO3 and BiVO4 under reductive H2 and NH3 atmospheres, respectively. Gong et al. (2021) and Wang et al. (2020) performed the deoxidation method under thermally inert atmospheres of N2 and vacuum, respectively, to achieve the introduction of oxygen vacancies in hollow hierarchical BiOCl and BiFeO3 nanobelt. Hetero-element doping (Peng et al., 2022), radio frequency sputtering (Shyam et al., 2020), mechanochemical force (A. Chen et al., 2021), crystal plane engineering (Liao et al., 2022a,b) and chemical reaction method (Kumar et al., 2021; G. Liu et al., 2022a) can also introduce OVs in oxygen-containing photocatalysts. However, these strategies have high energy consumption, high equipment cost, high equipment requirements, cumbersome steps, safety hazards or limited introduction of OVs. In contrast, adding biomass into the system for solvothermal synthesis of photocatalysts is a mild and fast strategy to introduce abundant OVs in the BiOCl lattices (Liao et al., 2021).

Biomass materials can be served as hard templates to modify the micromorphology of catalysts, as raw materials for top-down preparation of CQDs. Therefore, we reported a biomass material (PNP) to assist fabrication of BiOCl with special morphology and plentiful OVs. Liu and Zhang (2017) used PNP to fabricate CQDs with nitrogen doping (N-CQDs). Lu et al. (2022) claimed that N-CQDs improve electron transfer efficiency. Therefore, in this paper, PNP was added into the system for solvothermal fabrication of BiOCl to obtain CQDs/OVs-BiOCl with partial flower-like microsphere morphology, and the photocatalytic performance of the CQDs/OVs-BiOCl was significantly enhanced compared with the reference BiOCl. This work offers a meaningful reference for manufacture of highly productive photocatalyst for wastewater treatment.
All reagents were obtained from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory, and PNP was produced by Hongxiang Traditional Chinese Medicine Technology Co., Ltd.

Preparation process for the catalyst was shown in Scheme S1. Under vigorous stirring, 10 mL aqueous solution containing 0.86 g KCl was dripped into 40 mL glacial acetic acid with 5.59 g Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and desired PNP using a constant-flow pump at a constant speed. After stirring for 30 min, the suspension system was kept at 180 °C for 24 h in a hydrothermal reactor. After natural cooling, washing with deionized water and ethanol for several times and drying at 60 °C for 12 h, the product was ground to power to gain the catalyst. According to the PNP/BiOCl weight ratio (0, 0.5%, 1.0%, 1.5% and 2.0%), the correspondent photocatalyst was labelled as (0% PB, 0.5% PB, 1% PB, 1.5% PB and 2%PB), respectively. Similarly, CQDs were prepared by adding PNP and solvent (40 mL CH3COOH: 10 mL H2O) under the same synthetic conditions and the CQDs solids were obtained after evaporating the solvent.

Optical fluorescence properties of CQDs were investigated on an Agilent Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer under visible light. Using 200 nm UV light as the excitation wavelength and the excitation slit of 10 nm, the photoluminescence (PL) signal of the CODs solution produced by the hydrothermal treatment of PNP was tested. Using the visible light of 534 nm as the excitation wavelength, UCPL effect of the solution was detected. FT-IR of CQDs and 1.0%PB catalyst were detected on a PE Frontier FT-IR/NIR spectrometer. Specific surface area (SBET) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples were acquired on an SSA-4200 (Builder, China) and a D2 Phaser X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Cu Ka = 1.5406 Å), respectively. Microstructures of the catalysts and PNP were obtained on a Tescan vega 3 SBU scanning electron microscope (SEM). The elemental states and surface OVs on the sample surface were surveyed through an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo-Fisher Scientific) and electron spin resonance (ESR, Bruker E500) at low temperature, respectively. Separation properties of e−/h+ pairs yielded by the samples under light irradiation were studied on a surface photovoltage spectrometer (SPS) for the photo-voltage and electrochemical workstation (Zolix LSP-X500 -CHI660E) for the photo-current (PC) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The optical absorption characteristics of the catalysts were investigated using a UV–Vis diffuse reflection spectroscopy (DRS, HITACHI UH4150). Room-temperature ESR was employed to test the free radicals generated in the photocatalytic system. To measure the free radicals, 5,5-dimethyl-1- pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) was used as the capture agent. According to the sample: DMPO: water (or methanol), 10 mg: 75 μL: 1000 mL was added to a centrifuge tube after ultrasonication, and then was irradiated under a xenon lamp for 2 min. Water was added to test hydroxyl radicals, and methanol was added to test superoxide radicals.

Photocatalytic performance of the photocatalysts was assessed by detoxification of RhB under visible light (≥420 nm) and PFOA under a 125 W high-pressure mercury lamp with vigorous magnetic stirring. 50 mL simulated contaminant solution (10 mg/L) and photocatalyst (dosage = 1 g/L) were loaded into a photoreactor and stirred for 30 min in dark to build the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. At particular intervals, 6 mL turbid liquid was sampled and the supernatant was used for measurement. The absorbance of RhB was checked on a TU-1950 UV–Vis spectrophotometer, the concentration of F− was checked on a Thermo ICS1000 ion chromatography and PFOA were measured on an AB SCIEX QTRAP4500 liquid chromatograph mass spectrometer.

The optical properties of CQDs prepared from PNP were characterized for the PL and UCPL properties, respectively, and the results were displayed in Fig. 1 A and Fig. 1B. Fig. 1A shows that the PL signals are generated at 492 nm and 534 nm, which is blue light and green light, respectively. The inset of Fig. 1A shows that the PL of the CQDs is blue-green, corresponding to the PL spectrum. Fig. 1B shows the spectrum of the UCPL effect of CQDs. The visible light with 534 nm wavelength was used as the excitation wavelength, and the UCPL signal was detected at a wavelength of 381.5 nm. XRD patterns of CQDs was shown in Fig.S1. As can be seen from Fig.S1, diffraction peak of CQDs presents a broad peak, and the corresponding intensity is weak, which is not a narrow and sharp peak, indicating that CQDs are not crystalline. The highest peak is located at 22.7°. However, the peak of CQDs cannot be observed in 1.0% PB, since the CQDs are amorphous and the content of CQDs in the catalyst is low. Fig. 1A and B demonstrate that CQDs with UCPL can be obtained by a hydrothermal reaction using PNP, glacial acetic acid and water (40 mL:10 mL) as raw materials. From the inset of Fig. 1A, the reason for the insignificant PL signals in the aqueous solution after the BiOCl synthesis system with the addition of PNP is that the CQDs have been composited with BiOCl and have not been dispersed into the solution. Fig. 1C shows the FT-IR spectra of CQDs and 1.0%PB. The absorption peak at 3445 cm−1, 2924 cm−1, 1720 cm−1 and 1228 cm−1 can be attributed to vibration of –OH, C–H, CO and C–C, respectively. According to the report of Zhang et al. (2021), the peak at 1625 cm−1 and 1115 cm−1 can be assigned to the bending vibration of amino groups and C–N stretching vibration. The obvious peak at 1385 cm−1 can be well assigned to the D-band of CQDs (Lu et al., 2022). The peak of 524 cm−1 stems from the vibration of Bi–O. These evidences suggest that CQDs have been successfully coupled with BiOCl.

The SBET test results of the fabricated catalysts were shown in Table 1 . The SBET of CQDs/BiOCl is significantly higher than that of the blank BiOCl (0% PB) due to the large pore volume. The large pore volume of CQDs/BiOCl indicates the high specific surface area. High SBET can offer more reactive sites, therefore, it is predicted that the activity of CQDs/BiOCl will be improved compared with 0% PB. Additionally, high surface area will definitely induce more defects, which will affect the separation and transfer of the photoactivated charges, supported by ESR measurement results.

XRD patterns of the fabricated photocatalysts were recorded in Fig. 2 . The obtained patterns were compared with the PDF-2 database, and all the diffraction peaks are well matched to those of tetragonal BiOCl (PDF 073–2060). Shown in Fig. 2A, the peaks at 2θ = 12.03°, 25.93° and 33.53 can be attributed to the (001), (101) and (102) crystal planes, respectively. From the magnified Fig. 2B of the (001) crystal plane, it can be seen that the diffraction peaks of 0.50%PB, 1.0%PB, 1.5%PB and 2.0%PB shift to a high angle, and the shift degree of 1.0%PB is the largest. According to the Bragg formula (2dsinθ = nλ) (Blazhevich, et al., 2020), the lattice spacing on the (001) plane becomes smaller, and the (001) lattice spacing for 1.0% PB will be the smallest.

The microstructures of the prepared samples were observed via SEM. The 0% PB (Fig. 3 A) exhibits irregular sheet-like structures and disorderly packed 2D structures, while the 0.50%PB (Figs. 3B), 1.0% PB (Figs. 3C), and 1.5% PB (Fig. 3D) present 3D flower-like microspheres assembled by flakes. When light is irradiated on the flower-shaped microspheres, multiple reflections will occur on the catalyst surface. Obviously, the 3D structure will improve the residence time of light in the catalyst and improve the light usage efficiency. Comparison of Fig. 3A and E, the smaller size of the 1.0% PB flakes and the smaller catalyst particles result in a larger SBET for the samples, which is consistent with the BET test results. Fig. 3A shows that the flake thickness of the 0% PB is between 316 nm and 885 nm, while Fig. 3E shows that the flake thickness of 1% PB is between 117 nm and 258.5 nm, which is significantly smaller than that of 0% PB, confirming to the test results of XRD. According to the diffusion formula t = d2/k2D reported by Di et al. (2015) (where D is the diffusion coefficient of the e−/h+ pair, k is a constant, d is the particle size), since 1.0%PB has a smaller particle size, 1.0%PB will have higher photogenerated charge separation efficiency. To study the formation of microspherical BiOCl, SEM observation was also carried out for PNP. As shown in Fig. 3F, the surface of PNP is multi-dimensional, which plays a role of a hard templating agent in the hydrothermal preparation process.

Since addition of PNP has induced the change of the morphology of BiOCl, the microchemical states of the surface elements of the 0%PB and 1.0%PB photocatalyst were analyzed by XPS. Illustrated in Fig. 4 A, full XPS spectrum of the 0%PB and 1.0%PB sample shows that both of the samples contain Bi, O, and Cl, while there is a tiny peak exists around 400 eV, further indicating that the CQDs with N-doping have been incorporated with BiOCl. High resolution C 1s of 1.0%PB (shown in Fig. 4B) can be divided into three peaks located at 284.8, 286.3 and 288.6 eV, which represents CC, C–N/C–O and CO, respectively (Zhang et al., 2021). As can be seen in Fig. 4C and D, the binding energies of Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 and Cl 2p of the 1%PB all shift towards higher binding energies for 1% PB compared to 0% PB due to the strong interaction between BiOCl and CQDs (Lin et al., 2018). The microchemical state of O 1s also changes. Zou et al. (2021) and Qin et al. (2022) reported that the high-resolution O 1s can be composed of Bi–O, OVs, and adsorbed oxygen (Ab-O), which are located at 530.0 eV, 532.1 eV, and 533.2 eV, respectively. Thus, the relative proportions of Bi–O, OVs and Ab-O of the 0%PB sample is 45.4%, 27.6% and 27% (Fig. 4E), respectively, while for the 1.0%PB, the value is 36.3%, 36.8% and 26.9% (Fig. 4F), respectively. The results confirm that the 1.0%PB sample has richer OVs than the reference BiOCl, which also indicates that PNP plays an important role in the introduction of OVs.

Low-temperature ESR-OVs experiments were used to further verify the OVs in the samples, the results were shown in Fig. 5 . Qiu et al. (2020) reported that samples with stronger ESR signals generated by OVs had more OVs. Therefore, it can be concluded from Fig. 5 that the OVs in the post-processed samples with PNP have remarkably enhancement than 0%PB. OVs signals were detected in all the samples, and the signal intensity generated by OVs is 1%PB > 0.50%PB > 2%PB > 0%PB. The observation indicates that the OVs introduced into the catalyst gradually increase as the addition amount of PNP increasing from 0 to 1.0%, while the OVs introduced into the catalysts gradually decreases when the addition of PNP exceeds 1.0%. In other words, the amount of OVs introduced can easily be regulated by the amount of PNP added. OVs play an important role in the generation of •O2 − radicals and the efficiency of photo-generated charge separation during photocatalysis. Naturally, the photoinduced carriers separation behavior and the free radicals in the photocatalytic system will be investigated.

The photoinduced charge separation behavior of the samples under illumination was explored by SPS, PC response and EIS. The SPS test results of all the photocatalysts were presented in Fig. 6 A. All the CQDs/BiOCl photocatalysts exhibit more intensive SPS signals than the bare BiOCl under visible and UV light irradiation, which originates from the flower-like microsphere morphology, CQDs and OVs. OVs can capture electrons to achieve the effective separation for e−/h+ pairs, 3D microstructure effectively reduces the recombination probability of e−/h+ pairs, and CQDs can rapidly transfer electrons, ameliorating the separation of photoexcited carriers. It can be observed from Fig. 6A that 1%PB has the strongest SPS signal. According to the measurement principle of SPS (K. Xu et al., 2022), the 1%PB sample holds the highest charge separation efficiency. The SPS response of BiOCl in the 640–720 nm range originates from OVs, which provides strong evidence that a new energy level can be established between the valence band (VB) and the conduction band (CB) (M. Li et al., 2022; Arif et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2021), and electrons can be re-excited by visible light to reach the CB (Liang et al., 2019). According to E = 1240/wavelength, the position of the new energy levels can be obtained to be approximately 1.72–1.94 eV. The photoelectrochemical experimental (PC and EIS) results were presented in Fig. 6B and C. The results of the PC test with visible light in Fig. 6B show that the PC signals of the samples are in the same order as the SPS signals. From the Nyquist semicircle radius in Fig. 6C, it can be concluded that the AC impedance of each CQD/BiOCl is significantly lower than that of the blank BiOCl as reported by Dou et al. (2021). The decreased AC impedance is derived from CQDs and thin sheets of BiOCl, CQDs can transfer electrons like metals, thereby accelerating the photogenerated charge separation efficiency. Meanwhile, thin sheets of BiOCl can shorten the time from the bulk to the surface of BiOCl, displaying high separation of photoinduced carriers. The minimum Nyquist radius of 1.0%PB indicates that the EIS of the sample is minimal and favors the transfer of photoinduced charge.

The comprehensive test results of SPS, PC and EIS show that 1.0%PB holds the highest photoinduced charge separation efficiency. According to the amount of PNP added to the synthesis system, it can be seen that the amount of CQDs in 1.5%PB and 2.0%PB is higher than that in 1.0%PB. Combined with the results of ESR-OVs experiments, it can be seen that OVs play a more important role in promoting the photogenerated charge separation than CQDs.

The activity of photocatalysts is achieved through free radicals, so the study on the role of free radicals is important for exploring the mechanism of photocatalysis. Thus, room-temperature ESR-radical experiments were applied to investigate the types of radicals, and trapping experiments were employed to ascertain the role of active radicals in photocatalysis. As shown in Fig. 7 A, typical DMPO-•OH signals with four peaks and DMPO-•O2 − signals with six peaks were detected in the 1.0%PB photocatalytic system, indicating that both •OH and •O2 −can be generated in the photocatalytic system of 1.0%PB, according to the paper reported by Yang et al. (2022) and He et al. (2022). Fig. 7B records the change of the degradation efficiency of RhB over 0% PB and 1.0%PB after adding benzoquinone (BQ, trapping •O2 −), ammonium oxalate (AO, trapping h+) and isopropanol (IPA, trapping •OH) as scavengers (). From the trapping experimental results for the 0% PB photocatalytic system, it can be seen that •OH performs minor role for the degradation of RhB, while h+ and •O2 − play the major role, and holes take stronger role than •O2 −in destruction of RhB. However, in the photocatalytic degradation system of 1% PB. h+ and •O2 − play the main role in 1.0% PB photocatalytic system, but •O2 −plays more important role than h+, which can be assigned to abundant OVs in 1.0% PB. The effect of •OH in the degradation system is obviously enhanced due to the largely enhanced photogenerated charge separation efficiency. The results show that •O2 − radicals play the central role in photocatalytic degradation of RhB. NBT-•O2 − experiments were used to detect the •O2 −generation capacity among all the catalysts, and the results were given in Fig. 7C. All the CQDs/BiOCl composites exhibit higher •O2 − formation concentration than the single BiOCl, which coincides well with the e−/h+ segregation efficiency. Meantime, the lowest residual concentration of NBT in the 1.0%PB photocatalytic system suggests that the system generates the most •O2 −, indicating the photocatalytic activity will be the highest (J. Chen et al., 2021; H. Li et al., 2021).

RhB is a typical dye contaminant in wastewater, and to compare the activity of 1% PB with the activity of photocatalysts prepared by other research groups, RhB was selected as the pollutant simulated to investigate the activity of the prepared photocatalysts under visible light exposure. As shown in Fig. 8 A, the destruction efficiency of RhB is 36.9% over 0%PB under visible light irradiation for 20 min, while the degradation efficiency of RhB on 0.50%PB, 1.0%PB, 1.5%PB, and 2.0%PB is 93.4%, 96.7%, 90.7% and 87.6%, respectively. All the CQDs/BiOCl composites exhibit better photocatalytic activity than 0%PB, and 1.0%PB owns the best photocatalytic activity, which is consistent with the photoinduced charge segregation efficiency. High photoinduced charge separation efficiency is the leading driver for the promoted decomposition of RhB.

Another factor contributing to higher photocatalytic activity of CQDs/BiOCl is that CQDs/BiOCl holds larger SBET than the reference BiOCl. As shown in Fig. 8A, the adsorption removal of RhB on the bare BiOCl is 9.3%, while adsorption efficiency of RhB on the CQDs/BiOCl is more than 12.5%. High SBET increases the adsorption capacity of the catalyst, providing more active sites and facilitating heterogeneous photocatalytic reaction. Fig. 8B shows the evolution of RhB on 1.0%PB with irradiation time. The maximum absorption peak of RhB at 554 nm diminishes in intensity, signifying that RhB can be progressively degraded. The gradual blue-shift of the maximum absorption peak indicates that RhB can be disassembled gradually to form intermediates or small molecules. Fig. 8C suggests that the decay of RhB proceeds according to first-order kinetics, and the degradation rate constant can be fitted as ln(A0/At) = kt reported by Bunluesak et al. (2021), where k, A0 and At are the decay rate constant and the absorbance of RhB when irradiation time is 0 and t, respectively. The degradation activity of 0.5%PB, 1.0%PB, 1.5%PB, and 2.0%PB is 6.7 times, 7.6 times, 5.8 times and 5.2 times of that of 0%PB, respectively.

The C–F bond possesses extremely high bond energy, thereby exhibiting extremely stability. PFOA is basically non-degradable in the natural environment and is a persistent organic compound. Medical studies have confirmed links between PFOA and kidney cancer, testicular cancer, preeclampsia, thyroid disease, ulcerative colitis, high cholesterol and breast cancer, and PFOA can be passed to the next generation (Ducatman et al., 2015; Bartell and Vieira, 2021). In view of the high toxicity of PFOA to the human body, degradation of PFOA over the 1.0%PB sample was further evaluated under a 125 W mercury lamp irradiation using 0%PB as reference. Fig. 8D and E depict the degradation efficiency of PFOA (D PFOA) and the C–F cutting ability (DF) over 1.0%PB and 0%PB after 120 min illumination. The DPFOA and DF on the 1.0%PB is 80.4% and 13.7%, respectively, compared to 55.8% and 11.0% on 0% PB. This observation shows that the 1.0%PB sample still has a better degradation ability towards PFOA than the 0%PB photocatalyst. Fig. 8F illustrates that the decarboxylation of PFOA over the photocatalysts also follows first-order kinetics, and the degradation rate constant of PFOA on 1.0%PB is 1.8 times of that on 0%PB.

High stability of the catalyst is the basis for the intense practical applications. The stability of 1.0%PB was investigated by five-cycle experiments toward decay of RhB and XRD images of the sample after each cycle, and the results were shown in Fig. 9 A and Fig. 9B. The results show that 1.0%PB preserves excellent photocatalytic ability and phase stability.

To explore the flower-like microspherical CQDs/OVs-BiOCl prepared using PNP-assisted preparation whether or not has application prospects, the activity of 1.0%PB towards destruction of RhB was compared with that of BiOCl-based photocatalysts prepared with the different strategies by other groups (Lin et al., 2018; Mokhtari and Tahmasebi, 2021; Dong et al., 2021; Li et al., 2016; Jiang et al., 2022; Bai et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2019). As shown in Table 2 , the difference in SBET is not the main factor which affects the performance of photocatalysts, amongst all the factors which determine the photocatalytic activity, separation of the photoinduced carriers plays a leading role. The excellent photocatalytic performance of 1.0%PB indicates that it has potential application prospects.

A prerequisite for exploring the photocatalytic mechanism of CQDs/OVs-BiOCl is to understand the energy band structure of the samples, therefore the UV–Vis DRS of the samples were studied and the results were shown in Fig. 10 . As can be seen from Fig. 10, BiOCl has strong absorption in the ultraviolet region, but the absorption in the visible light region is significantly weaker than that in the UV light region. This is because there is an obvious difference between intrinsic and extrinsic absorption coefficients. The inset of Fig. 10 shows that the bandgap (Eg) of the photocatalyst is 3.27 eV. Owing to the presence of CQDs, proven by the results of Fig. 1, all the CQDs/BiOCl composites demonstrate significantly improved light absorption in UV–Vis light region compared to the reference BiOCl. The band structure of BiOCl can be obtained by Mulliken electronegativity theory as follows: EVB = X - Ee + 0.5 Eg, ECB = EVB – Eg, where Ee is the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale (about 4.50 eV, supported by Xiao et al., 2019), X is the electronegativity of the semiconductor BiOCl (about 6.34 eV, supported by Hu et al., 2020), EVB and ECB are the VB and CB edge potential. Therefore, the EVB and ECB is 3.47 eV and 0.20 eV, respectively.

Hence, the photocatalytic destruction mechanism of CQDs/OVs-BiOCl was proposed in Fig. 11 . Since the Eg is 3.27 eV, only UV light with a wavelength of below 379.2 nm (acquired via 1240/Eg) can excite electrons on VB to jump to the bottom of CB directly. However, after the establishment of new energy levels built by OVs. Electrons on VB can be easily excited to the new energy levels by visible light, and thus to the CB. Since the new energy levels are between 1.72 eV and 1.94 eV, therefore, photons with wavelengths of less than 604.9 nm can excite electrons on the new energy levels to CB. Furthermore, the excellent conductivity of CQDs makes it possible to rapidly transfer electrons to CQDs, enabling spatial separation from holes. Electrons on CQDs can react with O2 adsorbed on CQDs to form •O2 −(Yang et al., 2021). Besides, •O2 − can also be formed by electron combines with O2 activated by OVs (Liao et al., 2022). Therefore, O2 can be converted into •O2 − on CQDs and OVs, and the separated photogenerated charge pairs accelerates the generation of •O2 −. After electrons jump away from the VB, holes with potent oxidizing ability reserve on VB. OH− and H2O can be oxidized to form •OH by holes. •O2 −, •OH and h+ work together to promote the degradation of pollutants.

In this paper, flower-like microspheres CQDs/OVs-BiOCl composite photocatalysts were successfully synthesized in-situ by adding Panax notoginseng powder into the hydrothermal synthesis system of BiOCl. Panax notoginseng powder acts as templating agents, which significantly changes the microscopic morphology and surface properties of the photocatalysts. Panax notoginseng powder is also the raw material for forming of CQDs. The level of OVs can also be readily controlled by the dosage of Panax notoginseng powder. When the PNP/BiOCl mass ratio is 1.0%, the sample has the largest specific surface area and surface OVs, and the highest photogenerated charge separation efficiency. The destruction rate constant of RhB and PFOA on 1.0%PB is 6.6-fold and 0.8-fold higher than that on the reference BiOCl, respectively. The flower-like microsphere structure, CQDs and OVs synergistically promote the photocatalytic performance. This demonstration paves way for designing highly efficient photocatalyst for environmental purification.
Hongru Liao: Writing - original draft. Yu Ran: Investigation. Junbo Zhong: Funding acquisition. Jianzhang Li: Investigation. Minjiao Li: Investigation. Hao Yang: Investigation.
